region_of_origin: Tamil Nadu, India; widely consumed in Singapore

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP used as closest defensible match for cooked mutton/lamb dish. No Madras-specific curry code exists in FoodEx2 hierarchy.

main_ingredients: South Indian spices (cumin, coriander, chilli, turmeric, garam masala)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Madras Mutton is approximately 200 g, representing a standard restaurant or home-cooked portion of the curry with boneless meat pieces and sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Madras Mutton — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Foodo database entry for Madras Mutton (cr_id G00010258). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat not reported by HPB; estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked lamb shoulder (NDB 17290) as closest approved-source analogue.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on lamb/mutton curry with vegetables
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Madras Mutton in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on the analogous food category of meat-based curry with non-starchy vegetables (tomato, onion, bell pepper) and South Indian spices. The dominant carbohydrate contributors are tomato and onion, both low-GI foods. Protein (14.05 g/100g) and fat (11.58 g/100g) further attenuate glycaemic response. GL per 200 g serving = (35 × 7.44 × 2) / 100 = 5.2 g, classified as low. Estimation basis documented per Veritas GI policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 467 mg per 100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold; a 200 g serving delivers approximately 934 mg sodium, representing 41% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to 100 g or less, request reduced-salt preparation, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium dishes to keep total meal sodium below 800 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (7.44 g/100g), low GI (~35), and high protein (14.05 g/100g) collectively moderate postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Madras Mutton is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in a 150–200 g portion without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice; the high fat content (11.58 g/100g) should be monitored for long-term insulin sensitivity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~4.2 g/100g estimated) and cholesterol (85 mg/100g) from red meat raise LDL-cholesterol risk; sodium at 467 mg/100g adds blood-pressure burden.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Madras Mutton to once weekly, choose lean cuts, request preparation with minimal oil, and pair with high-fibre vegetables to partially offset the saturated fat and sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (mutton/lamb) is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which promotes pro-inflammatory cytokines TNF-alpha and IL-6; sodium at 467 mg/100g exacerbates tissue oedema.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit red meat consumption to once weekly or less, request preparation with olive oil rather than vegetable oil, and monitor tolerance to nightshade ingredients (tomato) which may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Madras Mutton is a South Indian-origin curry dish widely served in Singapore's Indian restaurants and hawker stalls. It consists of boneless pieces of mutton (lamb or goat) slow-cooked with fresh tomatoes, onions, and bell pepper in a richly spiced gravy featuring South Indian spices such as cumin, coriander, red chilli, turmeric, and garam masala. The dish has a deep reddish-brown colour, a robust and mildly fiery flavour profile, and a tender, succulent meat texture. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 192 kcal, 14.05 g protein, 11.58 g fat, and 7.44 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate dish. Sodium is elevated at 467 mg/100g, which is a concern for hypertensive individuals. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 35 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management when not paired with large portions of white rice. Cholesterol is 85 mg/100g, and saturated fat is estimated at approximately 4.2 g/100g from the red meat content. Madras Mutton is typically consumed as a main course at lunch or dinner, often accompanied by rice, roti prata, or naan. It is halal-certified in most Singapore establishments and is gluten-free by default. The dish is culturally significant in Singapore's Tamil and South Indian community and is a staple in Indian Muslim and Hindu restaurant menus across the island.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (canonical, reviewed, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked lamb as the closest approved-source analogue and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, clearly documented. All mandatory multilingual names present.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (4.2 g/100g) is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked lamb shoulder (NDB 17290) as HPB does not report saturated fat for this entry. This is documented in nutrient_source. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific Madras Mutton preparation are not reported in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for an equivalent composite dish as of 2026. 3. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Madras Mutton exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. Halal status assumed based on typical Singapore Indian restaurant preparation; individual establishments should be verified. 5. No zero-macro justification required as all core macros (protein_g 14.05, fat_g 11.58, carbohydrate_g 7.44) are non-zero. 6.
